content: Present Simple Tense: Used for habits, routines, and general truths. Structure: Subject + base verb (+ s/es for he/she/it). Examples: 'I eat breakfast at 8am.', 'She works in a hospital.', 'The sun rises in the east.' Negative: 'I don't like coffee.' / 'He doesn't play football.'

Present Continuous Tense: Used for actions happening now or around now. Structure: Subject + am/is/are + verb-ing. Examples: 'I am studying English.', 'She is cooking dinner.', 'They are playing football.' Negative: 'He is not working today.'

Past Simple Tense: Used for completed actions in the past. Structure: Subject + verb-ed (or irregular form). Examples: 'I went to school yesterday.', 'She watched a movie last night.', 'They played tennis on Saturday.' Negative: 'I didn't go to the party.' Questions: 'Did you enjoy the film?'

Future with 'will': Used for predictions, promises, and spontaneous decisions. Structure: Subject + will + base verb. Examples: 'I will call you tomorrow.', 'It will rain this weekend.', 'She will be late.' Negative: 'I won't forget.' Questions: 'Will you help me?'

Future with 'going to': Used for plans and intentions. Structure: Subject + am/is/are + going to + base verb. Examples: 'I am going to visit my parents next week.', 'She is going to start a new job.', 'They are going to move house.'

Present Perfect Tense: Used for past actions with present relevance or unfinished time periods. Structure: Subject + have/has + past participle. Examples: 'I have visited Paris three times.', 'She has lived here since 2020.', 'They have just arrived.' Negative: 'I haven't finished yet.'

Past Continuous vs Past Simple: Past Continuous describes a longer action in progress when something happened. Past Simple describes completed actions or interruptions. Examples: 'I was watching TV when the phone rang.' (continuous = watching, simple = rang), 'While she was cooking, the children were playing.'

Articles (a/an/the): 'A' and 'an' are indefinite articles for singular countable nouns (unspecified). 'The' is the definite article for specific things. Examples: 'I saw a dog.' (any dog), 'I saw the dog from next door.' (specific dog), 'She is an engineer.', 'The sun is bright.' No article for general plural/uncountable: 'I like dogs.'

Countable vs Uncountable Nouns: Countable nouns can be counted (one apple, two apples). Uncountable nouns cannot be counted individually (water, rice, information, advice). Use 'many' with countable, 'much' with uncountable. Examples: 'How many books do you have?', 'How much water do you need?', 'I need some information about the course.'

Plural Nouns: Regular plurals add -s or -es (cat → cats, box → boxes). Nouns ending in consonant + y change to -ies (baby → babies). Irregular plurals: child → children, man → men, woman → women, person → people, tooth → teeth, foot → feet, mouse → mice, fish → fish (same).

Possessive Forms: Add 's to singular nouns (the boy's book) and to plural nouns not ending in s (the children's toys). Add only ' to plural nouns ending in s (the students' classroom). For compound possession, add 's only to the last noun (John and Mary's house). Examples: 'That is Sarah's car.', 'The teachers' lounge is on the second floor.'

Prepositions of Place: in (enclosed space: in the room), on (surface: on the table), at (specific point: at the door), under (below), next to (beside), between (in the middle of two), behind, in front of, opposite. Examples: 'The book is on the table.', 'She lives in London.', 'Meet me at the station.'

Prepositions of Time: at (specific time: at 3pm, at midnight), on (days/dates: on Monday, on July 4th), in (months/years/seasons: in June, in 2023, in winter). No preposition with 'last', 'next', 'every': last week, next month, every day. Examples: 'The meeting is on Tuesday at 10am in the morning.'

Adjectives: Order in English follows OSASCOMP — Opinion, Size, Age, Shape, Color, Origin, Material, Purpose. Examples: 'a beautiful small old round red Italian leather handbag'. Comparative: adjective + -er (or 'more' + adjective for 2+ syllables). Superlative: adjective + -est (or 'most' + adjective). Examples: bigger, more interesting, the biggest, the most interesting.

Adverbs of Frequency: always (100%), usually (80%), often (70%), sometimes (50%), rarely/ seldom (20%), never (0%). Position: before the main verb but after 'be'. Examples: 'I always drink coffee in the morning.', 'She is usually on time.', 'They never eat meat.' Can also go at beginning/end: 'Sometimes I go jogging.'

Modal Verbs for Ability and Permission: can (ability/permission: I can swim, Can I leave early?), could (past ability/polite request: I could run fast when I was young, Could you help me?), be able to (ability in all tenses: I will be able to come tomorrow), may (formal permission/possibility: May I come in?, It may rain), might (weaker possibility: It might snow).

Modal Verbs for Obligation and Advice: must (strong obligation from speaker: I must study harder), have to (external obligation: I have to wear a uniform at work), should (advice: You should see a doctor), ought to (moral obligation/advice: You ought to apologize). Negative forms: mustn't (prohibition), don't have to (no obligation). Examples: 'You mustn't smoke here.' vs 'You don't have to come.'

Conditionals — Type 0 and 1: Type 0 (general truths): If + present simple, present simple. Example: 'If you heat water to 100°C, it boils.' Type 1 (real future): If + present simple, will + base verb. Example: 'If it rains tomorrow, I will stay home.' 'If you study hard, you will pass the exam.' Unless = if not: 'I won't go unless you come.'

Conditionals — Type 2 and 3: Type 2 (unreal present): If + past simple, would + base verb. Example: 'If I had a million dollars, I would travel the world.' Type 3 (unreal past): If + past perfect, would have + past participle. Example: 'If I had studied harder, I would have passed the exam.' Mixed: 'If I had eaten breakfast, I wouldn't be hungry now.'

Relative Clauses: Defining relative clauses give essential information (no comma). Non-defining give extra info (with comma). Relative pronouns: who (people), which (things), that (people/things, defining only), where (places), when (time), whose (possession). Examples: 'The woman who lives next door is a doctor.' (defining), 'My mother, who is 65, still works.' (non-defining), 'That's the restaurant where we met.'

Passive Voice: Used when the action is more important than who did it. Structure: Subject + be + past participle (+ by agent). Formed in any tense by conjugating 'be'. Examples: 'The report was written by Sarah.' (past passive), 'English is spoken worldwide.' (present passive), 'The house is being painted.' (present continuous passive), 'The work has been completed.' (present perfect passive).

Reported Speech: When reporting what someone said, shift tenses back. Present → Past: 'I am tired' → She said she was tired. Past/Present Perfect → Past Perfect: 'I saw him' → She said she had seen him. Will → Would. Can → Could. Time/place words change: today → that day, tomorrow → the next day, here → there. Questions use statement word order: 'Where do you live?' → He asked where I lived.

Question Formation: Yes/No questions invert subject and auxiliary: 'You are happy.' → 'Are you happy?' 'She can swim.' → 'Can she swim?' Wh- questions add question word before auxiliary: 'Where do you live?', 'What did she say?' If no auxiliary, add do/does/did: 'You like pizza.' → 'Do you like pizza?' Subject questions don't use do: 'Who wrote this book?' (not 'Who did write')

Tag Questions: Small questions added to the end of statements. Pattern: positive statement + negative tag (or vice versa). Use same auxiliary and pronoun. Examples: 'You're coming, aren't you?', 'She doesn't like fish, does she?', 'They've finished, haven't they?', 'It's cold today, isn't it?' Special: 'I am' → 'aren't I?': 'I'm late, aren't I?'

Gerunds vs Infinitives: Gerund (verb-ing) is used: after prepositions (interested in learning), as subject (Smoking is bad), after certain verbs (enjoy, finish, suggest, avoid, consider). Infinitive (to + verb) is used: after adjectives (happy to help), to express purpose (I came to learn), after certain verbs (want, decide, hope, plan, promise). Some verbs can take either with meaning change: stop to smoke (purpose) vs stop smoking (quit).

Subject-Verb Agreement: Singular subjects take singular verbs; plural subjects take plural verbs. Tricky cases: words between subject and verb don't change agreement ('The box of chocolates is on the table.'), each/every/either/neither are singular ('Each student has a book.'), collective nouns can be singular or plural depending on meaning ('The team is winning.' vs 'The team are arguing among themselves.').

Phrasal Verbs: Verb + particle(s) with meaning often different from the original verb. Separable: put on → put it on (object can go between). Inseparable: look after → look after them (object always after). Common: get up (rise), turn on/off (activate/deactivate), look for (search), give up (quit), run into (meet by chance), take off (remove / depart), put off (postpone), come up with (invent). Examples: 'I ran into an old friend yesterday.', 'Please turn off the lights.'

Comparatives and Superlatives: One-syllable adjectives add -er/-est (tall → taller → tallest). Two-syllable ending in -y: y → i + er/est (happy → happier → happiest). Two+ syllables: more/most + adjective (beautiful → more beautiful → most beautiful). Irregular: good → better → best, bad → worse → worst, far → farther/further → farthest/furthest. Use 'than' with comparatives: 'She is taller than me.'

Causatives (have/get something done): Used when someone else does something for you. Structure: have/get + object + past participle. Examples: 'I had my hair cut yesterday.' (the hairdresser cut it), 'She got her car repaired.', 'We are having the house painted next week.' Also: make/let/help + base verb: 'She made me clean my room.', 'Let me help you.'

Wish and If Only: Used to express regret or desire for something different. Wish/if only + past simple (for present unreal situations): 'I wish I had more free time.' (but I don't). Wish/if only + past perfect (for past regrets): 'I wish I had studied harder.' (but I didn't). Wish/if only + would (for complaints about habitual behavior): 'I wish you would stop smoking.' If only is more emphatic: 'If only I knew the answer!'
